explanation: The requirement means that the system shall do prop_0, and as part of that action (which 'shall' covers in requirements lingo), prop_1 must also happen. In formalization, this is often interpreted as both prop_0 and prop_1 must always (globally) hold.

The sentence 'The system shall prop_0 which shall prop_1' implies that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 shall also hold, i.e., prop_1 must hold at least whenever prop_0 does. This is captured by the implication globally: whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must be true as well.

The requirement is ambiguous, but typically, 'shall ... which shall ...' indicates that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must also occur (possibly at the same time). Assuming prop_1 is a necessary property whenever prop_0 holds: globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds as well.
This specifies that prop_0 and prop_1 must always hold, i.e., globally, both prop_0 and prop_1 are true.
The statement 'The system shall prop_0 which shall prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This is a requirement that prop_1 is true whenever prop_0 is true.

The sentence has two requirements: the system shall prop_0, and it shall prop_1. Both are to hold globally (that is, always). The requirements are independent and conjunctive, thus we use G (globally) on each and connect with logical AND.
This means that prop_0 must hold, and, in addition, prop_1 must also hold (due to the wording 'which shall'). If 'shall' is interpreted globally, then both must always hold.

This requirement structure typically means that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must also hold at that point. This is a safety property stating that 'prop_1' must always be true whenever 'prop_0' is true.
The statement requires that the system always satisfies prop_0, and whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at that moment. This can be captured by globally enforcing that prop_0 implies prop_1.

The phrase 'The system shall prop_0 which shall prop_1' indicates that whenever prop_0 holds, it must be the case that prop_1 also holds at the same time. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold: G(prop_0 -> prop_1).
